Step 6: The game will continue until one player is unable to make a move. The player who is unable to place a bishop on a non-attacked square loses.

Step 7: The first player can always force the second player to lose by following the strategy of placing bishops on squares that are not in the same diagonal as any previously occupied square. The first player will always have a move available until the second player is forced into a position where they cannot make a move without attacking an already occupied square.

Therefore, the first player wins the game with bishops.

Step 1: We start by analyzing the game with bishops on a chessboard.

- A chessboard has 64 squares.
- A bishop attacks along diagonals, so it can attack up to 14 squares (7 squares along each of the two diagonals).

Initially, no bishops are on the board, so the first player can place a bishop on any of the 64 squares. After that, the second player must place a bishop on a non-attacked square. This process continues until one player cannot place a bishop on a non-attacked square.

- Since each move places a bishop on a non-attacked square, and there are 64 squares, the maximum number of moves is 64.
- If both players play optimally, they will each place a bishop on a non-attacked square until all 64 squares are occupied.
- The player who places the 64th bishop wins.

Conclusion for part (a): The first player has a winning strategy. The first player can always place a bishop on a non-attacked square, and since there are an even number of squares, the first player will always be the one to place the 64th bishop.

Step 2: Now let's analyze the game with rooks on a chessboard.

- A rook attacks along rows and columns, so it can attack up to 14 squares (7 squares in each row and 7 squares in each column).

Initially, no rooks are on the board, so the first player can place a rook on any of the 64 squares. After that, the second player must place a rook on a non-attacked square. This process continues until one player cannot place a rook on a non-attacked square.

- Since each move places a rook on a non-attacked square, and there are 64 squares, the maximum number of moves is 64.
- If both players play optimally, they will each place a rook on a non-attacked square until all 64 squares are occupied.
- The player who places the 64th rook wins.

Conclusion for part (b): The first player has a winning strategy. The first player can always place a rook on a non-attacked square, and since there are an even number of squares, the first player will always be the one to place the 64th rook.

Final answer:
\boxed{\text{The first player has a winning strategy in both games.}}

The answer is \(\boxed{2 \text{ or } 3}\).
